Proving the Limit of a Sequence
Prerequisites
Proving the Limit of a Null Sequence
Introduction
?

In previous lessons, we saw how to define the limit of a null sequence (i.e., a sequence whose limit equals zero) and how to construct a formal proof that a given sequence is null.

In this lesson, we'll extend our understanding to include sequences that converge a finite (not necessarily zero) limit. If such a limit exists, it's usually denoted as

Consider the following sequence:

It's easy to see that the limit of this sequence is

This means that for any there exists a natural number such that the distance between and is smaller than for all In other words,

Let's draw a few sketches to illustrate this idea.

  • If we select then for all
  • If then for all
  • If then for all

The key idea is this: By making smaller and smaller, we can always find an index where the distance between and is smaller than for all

The Formal Definition of a Finite Limit
?

Based on the discussion so far, the notation means that if we take any arbitrarily small positive number the distance of each from is smaller than for sufficiently large

This gives us our definition of a finite limit:

For any real there exists a natural number such that for every natural number we have

We can illustrate the key idea using the diagram shown below.

For example, the statement

means the following:

For any real there exists a natural number such that for every natural number we have

We can make this definition more formal by explicitly including an implication:

For any real there exists a natural number such that for every natural number if then

Finally, by expressing the last statement using formal symbolic notation, we get the following:

|nn+1−1|<ε.
